title: Knedlíky
subtitle: Czech bread dumplings
excerpt: A log of flour, egg, milk, and breadcrumbs, boiled whole, then sliced into thick rounds. The defining accompaniment to Czech goulash and roast pork, dense, absorbent, and completely without parallel in European cooking.
sourceNotes: Houskové knedlíky (bread dumplings) are the most common form of Czech dumpling, eaten alongside guláš, svíčková (beef in cream sauce), and vepřo knedlo zelo. They are made from a thick batter of flour, egg, warm milk, fine breadcrumbs, and baking powder, shaped into a log and boiled whole, then sliced for serving. The breadcrumbs give the finished dumpling a slightly open, spongy texture ideal for absorbing sauces. They are not a dish in their own right but a vehicle, their whole purpose is to carry sauce.

content.text: Knedlíky are unlike any other dumpling. They are not stuffed, not shaped into small pieces, and not boiled in a broth. They are cooked as a single log in a large pot of salted water, then sliced at the table. The texture is dense but not heavy: the breadcrumbs give an open crumb that absorbs sauce efficiently, and the baking powder provides a slight lift that keeps the interior from being stodgy.

The dough should be just sticky enough to hold its shape when rolled into a log with wet hands. If it is very sticky, refrigerate for 30 minutes before shaping, the dough firms up considerably when cold.

Whisk the {{eggs}} and warm {{whole-milk}} together. Add the {{plain-flour}}, {{breadcrumbs-dried}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{baking-powder}}. Mix until a thick, soft, slightly sticky dough forms. Cover and rest at room temperature for 30 minutes.

Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a rolling boil. With wet hands, shape the dough into a smooth log about 6 cm in diameter and 25 cm long. Lower gently into the boiling water. Boil steadily for 18 to 20 minutes, turning the log carefully once halfway through with a slotted spoon. The log is ready when a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean and the surface feels firm. Remove and rest on a board for 5 minutes. Slice into 1.5 cm rounds with a sharp knife or a length of thread pulled through the log.

items.fix: Refrigerate the dough for 30 minutes before shaping. It firms up considerably when cold. Lower the log very gently with both hands rather than dropping it in
items.cause: Dough too wet or not rested long enough
items.symptom: Log falling apart when lowered into the water

items.fix: The log must be simmered (not boiled hard) for the full 18 to 20 minutes. Test with a skewer. It should come out clean. If uncertain, cook for a further 5 minutes
items.symptom: Knedlíky dense and gummy in the centre

items.fix: Ensure the water is at a rolling boil before the log goes in. Use a pot wide enough for the log to move freely
items.cause: Water not at a full boil when the log was added, or pot too small

content.text: Knedlíky are the bread of Czech cooking: the starch that accompanies everything from guláš to svíčková to roast pork. They appear at every traditional Czech meal. The sliced rounds, slightly spongy and still warm, sit in the sauce and absorb it completely. They are not interesting on their own; next to a rich stew or braise, they are exactly what is needed. Czech cooks make them without thinking, the recipe is memorised, the technique automatic.
